coredata.dc:title: Effect of chemical treatment and intrinsic resistance on the humidity sensitivity of pencil graphite sensing material coated on paper substrate
coredata.dc:creator: Choo, Thye Foo
Kok, Kuan Ying
Saidin, Nur Ubaidah
Mat Zali, Nurazila
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we have demonstrated the humidity sensitivity of graphite pencil marks sensing material drawn on paper. The fabricated sensing devices (sensors) were characterized for their humidity sensitivity by measuring the resistance changes across the pencil marks. In order to improve the humidity sensitivity of the sensing material, ethanol chemical treatment was applied on the pencil marks to increase their surface hydrophilicity. Sensors with different levels of intrinsic resistances were also prepared and tested. Results showed that the ethanol-treated sensors have better humidity sensitivities and the intrinsic resistances of the sensors were directly proportional to their humidity sensitivity. To evaluate this carbon-based sensing material, the humidity sensing characteristics of the pencil graphite were gauged with other forms of carbon materials that have been reported in previous studies, such as carbon quantum dots film, carbon nanoparticles, carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide and graphene.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphite-on-paper
Humidity sensor
Resistivity
Chemical treatment
Ethanol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of pencil graphite-on-paper humidity sensors
Characterization of pencil graphite-on-paper humidity sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the test setup used to evaluate the humidity sensitivity of sensor.

FESEM micrographs of a) blank paper; b) paper coated with pencil graphite; c) photographs of blank paper and paper coated with pencil graphite; d) XRD diffractogram and EDS spectrum of the paper coated with pencil graphite.
Water contact angle of a) graphite-coated paper substrate; b) graphite-coated paper substrate with ethanol treatment.

Humidity sensing responses of the two types of sensors (Eth-sensor and Norm-sensor) with various intrinsic resistances. (Equations in the graph represent the straight lines and R2 is the linear correlation coefficient of the line).
Schematic illustrating sensing mechanism of the pencil graphite-on-paper sensor toward humidity.
The relationship between the intrinsic resistances of sensors and their humidity sensitivities.
The change of humidity sensitivity of the sensors over 14 days.
Response and recovery times of a) Eth-sensor 4; b) Norm-sensor 1.
Properties of the present pencil graphite-on-paper humidity sensor and other carbon-based humidity sensors reported in the literatures.

row.entry: Carbon nanoparticles in ink on polyethylene terephthalate (PET)
Oxidized multi-walled carbon nanotubes on paper
Carbon nanotubes functionalized with carboxylic acid on paper
Carbon quantum dots film on glass

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Sensitivity of the sensor is calculated from the linear regime of the response values versus relative humidity plot.

head.ce:title: Effect of chemical treatment and intrinsic resistance on the humidity sensitivity of pencil graphite sensing material coated on paper substrate

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Malaysian Nuclear Agency (Nuclear Malaysia) Bangi, 43000 Kajang, Selangor, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Malaysian Nuclear Agency (Nuclear Malaysia) Bangi, 43000 Kajang, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we have demonstrated the humidity sensitivity of graphite pencil marks sensing material drawn on paper. The fabricated sensing devices (sensors) were characterized for their humidity sensitivity by measuring the resistance changes across the pencil marks. In order to improve the humidity sensitivity of the sensing material, ethanol chemical treatment was applied on the pencil marks to increase their surface hydrophilicity. Sensors with different levels of intrinsic resistances were also prepared and tested. Results showed that the ethanol-treated sensors have better humidity sensitivities and the intrinsic resistances of the sensors were directly proportional to their humidity sensitivity. To evaluate this carbon-based sensing material, the humidity sensing characteristics of the pencil graphite were gauged with other forms of carbon materials that have been reported in previous studies, such as carbon quantum dots film, carbon nanoparticles, carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide and graphene.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of flexible devices has recently gained much attention because of the recent advances in Internet of Things (IoT) technologies. These flexible devices, which include sensors, are considered to be the technological basis of the IoT [1]. Previous studies have fabricated these flexible humidity sensors based on sensing materials such as cellulose paper [2], polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) [3], polypyrrole [4], poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) [5], sodium niobate (NaNbO3) [6], cupric oxide (CuO) [7], as well as, various types of carbon materials such as carbon nanotubes [8–10], reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [11–13], graphene oxide (GO) [14,15] and graphene [16], either as stand-alone materials or in the form of composites.

Carbon nanoparticles [17], carbon nanotubes [10,18], graphene oxide [15], graphene [16], as well as pencil graphite [19,20] have been used as a stand-alone sensing material to fabricate humidity sensors with a flexible substrate. Zhao et al. [18] developed a full carbon-based humidity sensor fabricated on the paper substrate. The electrodes were hand-written by graphite pencils, and the sensitive layer was made up of oxidized multi-walled carbon nanotubes. Han [10] fabricated a humidity sensor on cellulose paper using single-walled carbon nanotubes functionalized with carboxylic acid. In both studies, the hydrophobicity of the carbon nanotubes was reduced by chemical treatments to increase the humidity sensitivity of the sensors. The hydrophilicity capacity of sensing materials has been shown to affect the humidity sensing characteristics of a sensor. The strong hydrophilicity capacity of humidity sensing materials can also contribute to the response speed of the humidity sensor [21].

Both Kanaparthi et al. [19,22] and Elangkovan et al. [20] have developed a pencil graphite/paper-based humidity sensor. Kanaparthi [19,22] fabricated a low-cost capacitive humidity sensor that utilized graphite pencil for drawing the interdigitated electrodes. Elangkovan et al. [20] fabricated a similar capacitive sensor in the form of interdigitated electrodes and parallel electrodes. However, all these fabricated pencil graphite/paper-based humidity sensors were only used pencil graphite for drawing the electrodes of the paper sensor while cellulose paper was used as the main sensing material. The cellulose paper can act as the sensing material of a humidity sensor [23], when it comes in contact with moisture, water molecules will be adsorbed to the hydroxyl groups on cellulose paper, thus increase the dielectric constant [19,22] and conductivity [20,24] of the sensor.

A carbon-based humidity sensor that uses graphite in pencil lead as sensing material and paper as a substrate has not been duly investigated. The humidity sensing characteristics of pencil graphite, such as sensing response, sensitivity, and response/recovery times, are also not well documented. Besides that, no effort has been made to improve the sensing performance of the material. Thus, this study aims to investigate the humidity sensing characteristics of graphite pencil marks as the sensing material, as well as the effect of ethanol treatment and intrinsic resistance on the sensitivity of the material. The humidity sensing characteristics of the pencil graphite will also be gauged with other forms of carbon materials that have been reported in previous studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of pencil graphite-on-paper humidity sensors
ce:section.ce:para: A graphite pencil with 2B graphite grading scale was used as the source of humidity sensing material. A white, non-glossy, hard cellulose paper (business card) was used as the substrate. Ethanol (95% purity) was purchased from John Kollin Chemicals, UK. Firstly, the pencil was used to draw a large patch (4 cm × 4 cm) of graphite pencil marks on the cellulose paper. The graphite coated paper was then cut into smaller pieces with size of approximately 1 cm × 1.5 cm to be used as the humidity sensors. For chemical treated sensor, a drop of ethanol of 50 μL was dispensed on the surface of the sensor and let it air-dried.

The microstructures of the paper substrate and the graphite-coated paper substrate (sensor) were characterized using a Zeiss GeminiSEM 500 field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM). Chemical composition was determined by the FESEM-EDS analysis. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the graphite-coated paper substrate was recorded by using PANalytical X′Pert PRO using monochromated CuKα radiation (λ = 1.54184 Å) and phase composition were analyzed by Rietveld refinement method. The water contact angle on the sensor surface was obtained from the image recorded by a digital camera.

To setup the humidity sensing test, two crocodile clips were attached to the paper sensor with approximately 0.5–1 cm gap between the two clips. The sensor was covered inside a transparent test chamber measuring 10 cm × 15 cm x 7 cm. A small commercial thermo-hygrometer (Xiaomi Mijia Bluetooth thermometer 2) was attached to the test chamber to measure the % relative humidity (% RH) inside the chamber. The sensor was characterized for its humidity sensitivity by measuring the resistance change across the two crocodile clips. Resistance measurements and data logging were performed using a microcontroller board (Arduino Leonardo) that was connected to a computer. Fig. 1 shows the schematic of the humidity sensing test setup.

A custom test procedure was used to test the humidity sensitivity of the sensors. In the procedure, a small Petri dish containing 25 g of calcium chloride dihydrate (Scharlau Chemie S.A.) as desiccant was placed and kept overnight inside the test chamber together with a wired test sensor. The relative humidity of the test chamber was monitored until it reached a stable humidity reading, both the relative humidity value and the corresponding resistance value were then recorded. After that, the Petri dish containing calcium chloride dihydrate was rapidly removed out by briefly opening the test chamber, again the relative humidity value and the corresponding resistance value were recorded once the relative humidity value had stabilized. The process was continued until the humidity inside the test chamber same as the humidity outside the test chamber. For testing in high humidity environment, small pieces of soaked paper towel were placed inside the test chamber in stages. In every stage, the relative humidity value and the corresponding resistance value were recorded once the relative humidity value had stabilized. The tests were performed at room temperature. The sensor humidity sensitivity in this study is defined as the resistance difference over the relative humidity difference (as shown in Eq. (1)).
(1) Humidity sensitivity = (R2 − R1)/(H2 − H1)
where, H2 is 82% RH and H1 is 46% RH. R2 and R1 are the corresponding sensor resistance at 82% RH and 46% RH, respectively. The response time is defined as the time it takes to reach its average final resistance, and the recovery time is the time taken by it to go back to its initial resistance (from 56/57 to 63 and back to 56/57% RH). For identification purpose, the ethanol-treated sensors and non-treated sensor were named as Eth-sensor and Norm-sensor, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of pencil graphite-on-paper humidity sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2a and b show the FESEM micrographs of the blank paper substrate and the paper substrate with coated pencil graphite (sensor). Graphite particles and flakes can be seen coated on the cellulose fibers of the paper substrate (Fig. 2b). Fig. 2c shows the white color paper substrate that was turned to black color after the pencil drawing. The coated paper was characterized using XRD and FESEM/EDS, the results are shown in Fig. 2d. The coated paper exhibits diffraction peaks of cellulose, graphite, calcite and quartz. Both calcite and quartz are common filler materials used in the paper production. The chemical elements of the coated paper are mainly carbon, oxygen with small amounts of calcium, silicon and aluminum.

The hydrophilicity of the sensor surface was evaluated using water contact angle test. When the contact angle of the water droplet with the surface (θ) decreases, hydrophilicity of the surface increases. The hydrophilicity of the sensor surface is directly related to the amount of water molecules in moisture that can bind with the surface. Fig. 3 shows the water contact angle of the graphite-coated paper substrate (Norm-sensor) and graphite-coated paper substrate with ethanol treatment (Eth-sensor), measured at 73° and 50°, respectively. This shows that the ethanol treatment has increased the hydrophilicity of the sensor.

Previous studies have indicated that graphite surfaces are hydrophobic with water contact angle within the range of 90–95° [25]. The studies also showed that fresh graphite surface was mildly hydrophilic and became hydrophobic upon exposure to ambient air. The hydrophobicity of graphite surface was reported as the result of airborne hydrocarbons contamination [26,27]. Our study suggests that the ethanol chemical treatment might effectively reduce the hydrocarbons contamination on the graphite particles surface. As a result of this decontamination, the hydrophilicity of the sensor surface increases and this allows more water molecules to bind and react with the sensor surface.

Two types of sensors (Eth-sensor and Norm-sensor) with various intrinsic resistances were prepared and tested. Fig. 4 shows the sensing responses of the sensors at different relative humidity conditions, the responses of the sensors were recorded as the change in electrical resistance. It can be seen from this figure that the resistance increases with increasing relative humidity. Few mechanisms have been proposed by previous studies to explain this resistance change. For example, Alrammouz et al. [15] suggested that the interaction between a carbon-based material and water molecules depends on the presence of hydrophilic functional groups on its surface. When it makes contact with water molecules, the water molecules will bind with the hydrophilic functional groups by withdrawing electrons from its surface. As a result, it becomes less conductive. Duan et al. [17] analyzed the electrical conduction process of the adjacent carbon nanoparticles in the presence of water molecules. The study proposed that at dry condition, the electrons can easily pass through the adjacent carbon nanoparticles using tunneling effect. But with the increase of the adsorbed water molecules, the formation of condensed liquid water molecular membrane on the surface of the carbon nanoparticles hinders the electron tunneling. The pencil graphite-on-paper humidity sensor is suggested to follow a similar sensing mechanism which illustrated schematically in Fig. 5. In the absence of water molecules on the graphite particles surface, an electric current can easily flow through them, thus a low resistivity value is recorded. In high humidity environment, the graphite particles surface is saturated with water molecules, making an electric current harder to flow through them, hence a high resistivity value.

Results from Fig. 4 shows that the humidity sensing responses of all the sensors that were tested at 45–82% relative humidity conditions are good. The resistance changes are directly proportional to the humidity with a positive linear correlation coefficient ranging from 0.97 to 0.99. Results also show that the humidity sensitivities of the sensors are noticeably improved by the ethanol chemical treatment. The humidity sensitivity of sensors is represented by the gradient of individual straight-line graph in Fig. 4. Eth-sensors have a higher humidity sensitivity ranging from 2 to 8.57 kΩ/%RH, while Norm-sensors are generally lower, ranging from 1.36 to 2.37 kΩ/%RH. As mentioned earlier, the ethanol treatment might reduce the hydrocarbons contamination on the graphite particles surface, which allows more water molecules to bind and react with the sensor surface.

The humidity sensitivities of sensors with different levels of intrinsic resistances were also studied. The intrinsic resistances of these sensors were adjusted by varying the gap width between the two crocodile clips. Generally, a wider gap creates higher intrinsic resistance sensor. Fig. 6 shows that there is a direct proportion between the intrinsic resistances of the sensors and their humidity sensitivities. Highest humidity sensitivity of 8.57 kΩ/%RH was measured in Eth-sensor 2 that has the highest intrinsic resistance of 338 kΩ (at 46% RH), while Eth-sensor 4 (121 kΩ at 44% RH) has the lowest humidity sensitivity of 2.01 kΩ/%RH. The same trend can be seen in Norm-sensors. The high intrinsic resistance sensors have more graphite particle-particle surface contacts than the low intrinsic resistance sensors. This suggests that when these graphite particle-particle surface contacts were bound with water molecules, the movement of electrons across the surface contacts becomes restricted, and thus the increment of sensor resistance is significantly amplified.

Fig. 7 shows the typical change of humidity sensitivity of the sensors over time. Generally, the humidity sensitivity of the sensors gradually decreases over time. The Eth-sensor shows a higher sensitivity reduction of 10.5% over 14 days as compared to the Norm-sensor (1.8%). This can be ascribed to the recontamination of the treated graphite surfaces of Eth-sensor.

Although all the sensors have good linear responses and good sensitivities in humidity sensing (Fig. 4), these sensors share a common weakness, which is long response and recovery times. Fig. 8 shows the typical response and recovery of Eth-sensor and Norm-sensor under humidity changes. Eth-sensor 4 shows a response time of 270 s and a recovery time of 418 s (measured from 57% to 63% RH), while Norm-sensor 1 shows a response time of 236 s and a recovery time of 388 s (measured from 56% to 63% RH). There are no statistically significant differences in the response and recovery times between Eth-sensors and Norm-sensors, which range from 215 to 296 s in response times and 356–462 s in recovery times. Sensor response and recovery times are particularly important as a slow or sluggish sensor is inapplicable to fast humidity changing environment.

Table 1 summarizes and compares the properties of some of the existing carbon-based humidity sensors. It can be seen that the humidity sensors produced in this work and by Zhao et al. [18], Han et al. [9] and Alrammouz et al. [15] have long response or recovery times. The response and recovery times of the sensor fabricated by Zhao et al. [18] using oxidized multi-walled carbon nanotubes were around 5 min and 7 min respectively. Han et al. [10] fabricated a humidity sensor on cellulose paper using single-walled carbon nanotubes functionalized with carboxylic acid. The sensor had an excellent response time of 6 s (from 10 to 60–10% RH) but the recovery time was 120 s. Alrammouz et al. [15] fabricated a capacitive humidity based on graphene oxide sheets on a paper substrate. The response and recovery times of the sensor were around 500 s and 750 s, respectively (from 30 to 90–30% RH). Their study concluded that the performance of sensor was limited by its slow recovery, but an operating temperature of 50 °C will accelerate the recovery of the sensor. In recent studies, a flexible humidity sensor was fabricated by Duan et al. [17] using carbon ink that composed of carbon nanoparticles. Different from the humidity sensors mentioned above, the sensor [17] exhibited fast response and recovery times (56 s and 14 s, respectively). A single-layered carbon material, graphene, has also been fabricated into a humidity sensor. Khalifa et al. [16] fabricated a humidity sensor based on cellulose paper coated with graphene. This sensor also exhibited very fast response and recovery times (9 s and 15 s, respectively). This comparison suggests that the carbon nanostructures have a positive effect on the response/recovery times of the sensor.

The sensitivity of the carbon-based humidity sensors reported in the literatures is ranging from 0.4% to 8%/%RH. The pencil graphite-on-paper sensors in this study have a humidity sensitivity ranging from 0.5% to 2.5%/%RH, which is within that range. It is interesting to note that the sensors made from graphene [16], carbon nanotubes [9] and carbon quantum dots film [28] have a humidity sensitivity of 2.8%/%RH, 6%/%RH and 8%/%RH, respectively. It suggests that the carbon nanostructures also have a positive effect on the humidity sensitivity of the sensor.

This study has demonstrated that the pencil graphite on paper substrate has good linear response and good sensitivity in humidity sensing, and it is possible to further improve its humidity sensitivity with the aid of a simple ethanol treatment or an intrinsic resistance adjustment. This study suggests that the ethanol treatment reduces the amount of hydrocarbons on the surface of graphite particles, which leads to an increase of hydrophilicity of the sensor surface. A high hydrophilicity sensor surface allows more water molecules to bind with its surface by withdrawing electrons from it, and this causes a large increase in the sensor resistance. This study also suggests that the high intrinsic resistance sensor has more graphite particle-particle surface contacts than the low intrinsic resistance sensor. When these surface contacts are bound with the water molecules, the sensor resistance increment is significantly amplified. This work opens up new possibilities for the improvement of carbon-based humidity sensors.

T.F. Choo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. K.Y. Kok: Writing – review & editing. N.U. Saidin: Methodology, Formal analysis. N. Mat Zali: Methodology, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Materials capability and device performance in flexible electronics for the Internet of Things
J. Mater. Chem. C

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Atmospheric contaminants on graphitic surfaces
Carbon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Martinez-Martin, R. Longuinhos, J.G. Izquierdo, A. Marele, S.S. Alexandre, M. Jaafar, J.M. Gómez-Rodríguez, L. Bañares, J.M. Soler, J. Gomez-Herrero. Atmospheric contaminants on graphitic surfaces. Carbon 61 (2013) 33‐39.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive humidity sensing properties of carbon quantum dots films
Mater. Res. Bull.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhang, H. Ming, R. Liu, X. Han, Z. Kang, Y. Liu, Y. Zhang. Highly sensitive humidity sensing properties of carbon quantum dots films. Materials Research Bulletin 48 (2013) 790‐794.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Choo Thye Foo received his BEng and MSc from Universiti of Malaya in 2001 and Universiti Putra Malaysia (UPM) in 2012, respectively. Choo Thye Foo started his career as a research officer at the Malaysian Nuclear Agency (Nuclear Malaysia) beginning in 2002 until the present. His research interest is in the field of functional nanomaterials and advanced ceramic engineering. He is the first author of eight SCI indexed journal research papers. Two of his XRD diffraction patterns produced from the research he conducted have been accepted as reference patterns in International Centre for Diffraction Data (ICDD) ‘Powder Diffraction File’. He has also served as a reviewer for several SCI indexed journals, such as Materials Today Communications, Energy & Fuels, Journal of Cleaner Production, Analytica Chimica Acta and ACS Omega.
